critique.summary: The article presents an interactive 3D simulator for the three-body problem that runs in a web browser. It allows users to explore the complex dynamics of three gravitationally interacting bodies with adjustable parameters.
mainPoints: The simulator models the three-body problem in three dimensions.
It is accessible directly in a web browser without additional software.
Users can adjust parameters such as gravitational constant, softness, and time step.
The tool uses numerical integration methods to simulate the system's evolution.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about the existence of an interactive 3D three-body problem simulator in the browser are accurate and verifiable by visiting the provided URL. The three-body problem is a well-known classical physics problem, and browser-based simulations using numerical integrators are common and feasible. The parameters mentioned (gravitational constant, softness, time step) are standard in such simulations. However, the article lacks detailed explanations or references to the underlying numerical methods or validation of the simulator's accuracy.
considerations: The article does not mention which numerical integration method is used or its stability and accuracy.
There is no discussion of the limitations or potential numerical errors in the simulation.
The source or authorship of the simulator is not detailed, which could affect trust in its scientific rigor.
No comparisons to other existing three-body problem simulators or benchmarks are provided.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around an interactive 3D three-body problem simulator, with users commenting on the choice of numerical solvers and their effects on the simulation's behavior. Some users appreciate features like JSON export, while others note limitations or express interest in alternative methods.

comments.author: hazrmard
comments.text: Very cool! Interesting how the choice of solver affects the solution. Euler doesn't handle misbehaved equations very well. You can see this in the Helix setup where the bodies just fly off.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly identifies the limitations of the Euler method in numerical simulations, especially for chaotic systems like the three-body problem, making it a valuable insight.

comments.author: westurner
comments.text: Notes re: Multi-body problems on "Show HN: Browser-based interactive 3D Three-Body problem simulator" (2025) https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=45981336 which has Velocity Verlet integration method, 4th-order Runge-Kutta (RK4), and Kepler solvers
comments.analysis: This comment provides useful references to alternative numerical methods that are commonly used in physics simulations, indicating a good understanding of the topic.

comments.author: ubezon
comments.text: Very cool! nice to be able to export json too
comments.analysis: This comment expresses positive sentiment about the project and highlights a practical feature, though it lacks technical depth.

comments.author: hermitcrab
comments.text: Random3D seems to always have all the stars heading off to infinity.
comments.analysis: The observation about the simulation behavior is plausible given the chaotic nature of the three-body problem, but lacks detailed explanation.

comments.author: kordlessagain
comments.analysis: This comment is brief and unclear, likely referencing a missing feature or method without elaboration, thus providing little value.

comments.text: Had a similar idea and built a prototype for it. :)
comments.analysis: While showing enthusiasm, this comment adds no technical insight or critique relevant to the discussion.
